headline: A Teenager's Hurricane Safety Program Is Spreading Across Schools
meta_description: 16-year-old Elise Raurell created Storm Smart to teach South Florida students hurricane preparedness—and now over 500 students have learned it.
summary_short: After noticing her school never taught hurricane safety, a South Florida teen developed Storm Smart, a curriculum that's now reaching hundreds of students.
body:
Elise Raurell noticed something troubling growing up in South Florida: despite living in a hurricane-prone region, her public school taught nothing about hurricane preparation or safety. No guidance on assembling emergency kits, no warnings about standing water near downed powerlines, no advice about avoiding glass windows during storms.

So the 16-year-old decided to fill the gap herself. Last year, as her Girl Scout Gold Award project, Raurell created Storm Smart—a mini curriculum designed to slip seamlessly into existing school lessons. Rather than building something rigid and difficult to implement, she made the modules flexible enough for any classroom to adopt.

But Raurell's real insight was recognizing the power of peer teaching. Storm Smart allows students to learn the material and then teach it to their classmates, developing leadership skills while spreading critical safety information.

The results speak for themselves. Over 500 students have already received Storm Smart materials, some directly from Raurell, but many more from fellow students who stepped up to share what they'd learned. "One of the most rewarding parts of this program," Raurell explained, "is seeing students learn the material and then go on to teach their peers about it."

Storm Smart resources are now available online, ensuring that hurricane preparedness knowledge reaches beyond South Florida's classrooms.
